claims.text: If China is a threat to the UK, why does the UK keep sailing warships near China's coast? Sounds like provocation, not defence.
claims.ambiguity_note: Security vs power projection - aggressor reversal without factual break

claims.text: Britain talks about security threats, but most instability in the world today traces back to British colonial borders and wars.
claims.ambiguity_note: Historical responsibility != current threat - moral delegitimization

claims.text: Maybe China isn't a 'security threat' at all — maybe Britain is just afraid it will have to return what it stole.
claims.ambiguity_note: Restitution is real, threat assessment gets reframed as motive

claims.text: How can China be a national security threat when the UK economy depends on Chinese trade, tech, and manufacturing?
claims.ambiguity_note: Interdependence != security risk excluded - false incompatibility

claims.text: Funny how China is a 'threat' now, but Saudi Arabia or Israel never seem to be, despite their records.
claims.ambiguity_note: Double standards exist - whataboutism with real core

claims.text: China has never attacked the UK, never threatened invasion, never fired a missile at Britain — so what exactly is the 'threat'?
claims.ambiguity_note: Threat != kinetic war - reduction to open violence

claims.text: This just feels like the West recycling Cold War paranoia, this time projecting it onto China.
claims.ambiguity_note: Narrative framing vs intelligence assessment - psychological dismissal

claims.text: China never colonised Britain, never imposed sanctions, never bombed London — yet Britain calls China a threat?
Labeling China a threat is convenient when your own economy is failing and public trust is collapsing.
claims.ambiguity_note: Political incentives exist - cynical intent attribution

claims.text: Every major power uses influence and intelligence — why is it 'threatening' when China does it but 'normal' when the UK does?
claims.ambiguity_note: Espionage is universal - normalisation of scale and intent

claims.text: The 'rules-based international order' only seems to apply when non-Western countries break the rules.
claims.ambiguity_note: Selective enforcement is real - undermining legitimacy of norms

claims.text: Calling China an 'epoch-defining threat' sounds more like rhetoric than evidence. Big words don't equal real danger.
claims.ambiguity_note: Language vs substance - undercutting documented assessments
